---
title: "Process & filter samples"
description: "Cell filtering, doublets, normalization, and highly variable features."
categories: [openpipeline]
order: 20
---

[`openpipeline`]{.pkg-tag .pkg-openpipeline}<span class="swatch openpipeline"></span>

Processing takes the per-sample `.h5mu` files from
[ingestion](ingest-10x.qmd) and produces a clean, normalized dataset with a PCA
embedding — ready for [integration](integrate.qmd) and
[annotation](annotate.qmd).

The recommended entry point is **`process_samples`**, which wraps per-sample QC,
concatenation, normalization, and dimensionality reduction into one command. The
sections below explain each step so you can tune it.

## What single-sample QC removes

The count matrix from ingestion contains more than real cells:

- **Empty droplets** — barcodes that captured ambient RNA rather than a cell
  (very low counts).
- **Dying or damaged cells** — low total counts with a high fraction of
  mitochondrial RNA, which leaks into the cytoplasm when the membrane is damaged.
- **Doublets** — two cells captured together and sequenced as one, appearing as
  a barcode with roughly double the expected depth.

`rna_singlesample` applies per-sample QC filters to remove these:

```bash
nextflow run openpipelines-bio/openpipeline \
  -r 4.2.0 -latest -profile docker \
  -main-script target/nextflow/workflows/rna/rna_singlesample/main.nf \
  --id sample_A \
  --input sample_A.h5mu \
  --output sample_A_filtered.h5mu \
  --min_counts 500 \
  --max_counts 50000 \
  --min_genes_per_cell 200 \
  --var_name_mitochondrial_genes "mitochondrial" \
  --max_fraction_mito 0.2 \
  --publish_dir results/
```

:::{.callout-tip}
QC thresholds are dataset-specific. Inspect the count distribution
(`total_counts`, `n_genes_by_counts`, `pct_counts_mt`) before choosing cutoffs.
A common first pass is to plot them as violin or scatter plots and apply
thresholds at natural breakpoints.
:::

For CITE-seq data (RNA + ADT), run `prot_singlesample` alongside
`rna_singlesample` to apply CLR (Centered Log-Ratio) normalization to the `prot`
modality.

## Normalization and dimensionality reduction

After filtering, samples are concatenated and normalized together.
`rna_multisample` performs:

1. **Concatenation** — all per-sample files are joined into one object.
2. **Normalization** — each cell is scaled to a fixed total count (e.g. 10,000)
   so cells are comparable regardless of sequencing depth, then log1p-transformed
   to stabilize variance.
3. **Highly variable feature (HVF) selection** — most genes do not vary
   meaningfully across cells. Focusing on the 2,000–3,000 most variable genes
   retains biological signal while cutting noise and compute.
4. **PCA** — compresses the high-dimensional expression space into a smaller set
   of components capturing the major axes of variation.

```bash
nextflow run openpipelines-bio/openpipeline \
  -r 4.2.0 -latest -profile docker \
  -main-script target/nextflow/workflows/rna/rna_multisample/main.nf \
  --id cohort_01 \
  --input "results/sample_*_filtered.h5mu" \
  --output cohort_01_multisample.h5mu \
  --publish_dir results/
```

## Run the full pipeline in one command

`process_samples` wraps `rna_singlesample`, `prot_singlesample`, concatenation,
and `rna_multisample` into a single workflow — no need to chain steps manually:

```bash
nextflow run openpipelines-bio/openpipeline \
  -r 4.2.0 -latest -profile docker \
  -main-script target/nextflow/workflows/multiomics/process_samples/main.nf \
  --param_list samples.csv \
  --publish_dir results/
```

For large cohorts, `process_batches` splits samples into groups, runs the
multisample steps on each group in parallel, then merges — reducing peak memory.
See [Process many samples](process-many-samples.qmd) for the `param_list` format.

**Next:** [Integrate with Harmony or scVI →](integrate.qmd) ·
[every parameter →](../reference/index.qmd)
